top5Opportunities.statement: Increase the ability to detect an emergency exit door being propped open or activated within 60 seconds of the event

Increase the ability to verify that all emergency exit security devices across a portfolio are in functional alarm-ready state before a scheduled fire safety inspection without manual per-door testing
Increase the ability to detect approaching battery depletion in emergency exit monitoring devices before failure
Increase the ability to generate complete emergency exit compliance documentation meeting fire marshal requirements at any time without manual data entry
Minimize the likelihood of an emergency exit security device failing to trigger an alarm upon unauthorized door activation

needs.statement: Increase the ability to detect an emergency exit door being propped open or activated within 60 seconds of the event, measured as mean detection lag from door-open event to operator notification
needs.importanceRationale: Sub-60-second detection enables immediate response to unauthorized exit use, theft, or security breach before damage occurs or the individual leaves the property.
needs.satisfactionRationale: A1 mechanical alarms (40%) + A5 manual patrol (30%) = 70% of market with zero real-time detection. Detection lag is measured in hours, not seconds.

needs.statement: Increase the ability to verify that all emergency exit security devices across a portfolio are in functional alarm-ready state before a scheduled fire safety inspection, measured as confirmed-active device rate achievable without manual per-door physical testing
needs.importanceRationale: Pre-inspection verification avoids discovering non-functional devices during fire marshal visits, which triggers immediate compliance findings.
needs.satisfactionRationale: Only dormakaba SafeRoute (25%) provides electronic status verification. A1 mechanical (40%) and A5 patrol (30%) require manual per-door physical testing.

needs.statement: Increase the ability to detect approaching battery depletion in emergency exit monitoring devices before failure, measured as percentage of battery replacements performed before the device becomes non-functional
needs.importanceRationale: Battery failure silently disables monitoring; proactive depletion detection enables planned replacement before the device becomes non-functional.
needs.satisfactionRationale: No incumbent provides proactive battery depletion trending. A1 mechanical alarms have no battery. A3 SafeRoute is mains-powered. Battery monitoring is a fundamental gap.

needs.statement: Increase the ability to generate complete emergency exit compliance documentation meeting fire marshal requirements at any time without manual data entry, measured as documentation completeness score achievable at an unannounced inspection
needs.importanceRationale: Fire marshal inspections are unannounced; the ability to produce complete documentation on demand is critical for avoiding compliance findings.
needs.satisfactionRationale: A1 + A5 (70% market share) require fully manual paper records. A3 SafeRoute provides some logging but requires integration effort for compliance formatting.

needs.statement: Minimize the likelihood of an emergency exit security device failing to trigger an alarm upon unauthorized door activation, measured as false-negative alarm rate per 1,000 activations
needs.importanceRationale: A false-negative means the door was opened unauthorized without any alarm — the most critical failure mode for exit security devices.
needs.satisfactionRationale: Recalibrated from 6 to 5.5: A5 manual/do-nothing (30% share) has zero alarm hardware, pulling market-weighted satisfaction below initial estimate. A1 mechanical (40%) has reliable trigger mechanism.

needs.statement: Increase the ability to monitor real-time emergency exit door status across an entire commercial property portfolio from a single operator dashboard
needs.importanceRationale: Portfolio-level visibility enables centralized operations and proactive management across multiple properties without per-building physical presence.
needs.satisfactionRationale: Only A3 SafeRoute (25%) provides any form of dashboard monitoring. 75% of the market has zero remote portfolio visibility.

needs.statement: Minimize the likelihood of a building operator discovering an emergency exit compliance failure for the first time during a scheduled fire marshal inspection rather than through proactive monitoring
needs.importanceRationale: Discovering compliance failures at inspection creates immediate regulatory exposure, reputational damage, and potential occupancy restrictions.
needs.satisfactionRationale: A1/A5 dominant market (70%): proactive failure detection is non-existent. Compliance failures are structurally discovered at inspection time.

needs.statement: Minimize the likelihood of EasyWave protocol dependency requiring full hardware replacement when a commercial property portfolio transitions to an open-standard BMS platform (KNX or BACnet)
needs.importanceRationale: BMS platform migration is a predictable lifecycle event for commercial portfolios; protocol lock-in forces hardware replacement instead of gateway reconfiguration.
needs.satisfactionRationale: EasyWave is a proprietary protocol with no migration path to BACnet/KNX. Hardware replacement is the only option during BMS platform transitions.

needs.statement: Minimize the likelihood of emergency exit security installation requiring permanent electrical infrastructure modifications to building fabric
needs.importanceRationale: Permanent electrical modifications require building permits, tenant coordination, and create costs that scale with portfolio size.
needs.satisfactionRationale: A3 SafeRoute (25%) requires mains wiring and conduit per door. A1 mechanical (40%) needs no electrical work. Market-weighted satisfaction is low due to A3's wiring requirement.

needs.statement: Increase the ability to detect emergency exit monitoring device wireless connectivity failures before the next scheduled inspection cycle
needs.importanceRationale: Connectivity failures between inspections mean exits appear monitored but events are not transmitted — a silent security gap.
needs.satisfactionRationale: A1 mechanical (40%) has no connectivity. A5 patrol (30%) has no devices. Wireless connectivity monitoring is available only in IoT systems with limited deployment.

needs.statement: Minimize the likelihood of annual fire safety audit preparation requiring manual compilation of exit inspection records across multiple properties
needs.importanceRationale: Portfolio lessors manage multiple properties; manual per-property compilation multiplies audit preparation labor with portfolio size.
needs.satisfactionRationale: A1 + A5 (70%) require fully manual paper records across every property. No automated multi-property audit compilation exists.

needs.statement: Increase the ability to produce portfolio-level emergency exit compliance evidence on demand during fire marshal inspections or insurance renewals
needs.importanceRationale: Insurance renewals and fire marshal inspections require on-demand evidence; inability to produce documentation immediately creates premium surcharges or compliance findings.
needs.satisfactionRationale: No incumbent provides on-demand portfolio-level compliance evidence generation. Manual assembly from property-level records is required.

needs.statement: Minimize the likelihood of an emergency exit security device becoming non-functional during a building power interruption
needs.importanceRationale: Power interruptions are the highest-risk scenarios; exit security must remain functional to prevent unauthorized access during building system failures.
needs.satisfactionRationale: Mechanical alarm A1 (40%) operates at 0V — fully power-independent. A3 SafeRoute has UPS options. Satisfaction is high at 8/10; this need is already well-served.

needs.statement: Minimize the likelihood of emergency exit monitoring losing BMS communication following network infrastructure updates
needs.importanceRationale: BMS firmware and network updates are routine in commercial properties; monitoring disconnection after updates leaves exits unmonitored until manual reconnection.
needs.satisfactionRationale: A3 SafeRoute uses LON/BACnet with established update procedures. Wireless IoT systems with proprietary protocols are more vulnerable to integration breakage after updates.

needs.statement: Increase the ability to provide asset managers with proactive emergency exit compliance status updates without manual report generation
needs.importanceRationale: Asset managers require compliance visibility across their portfolio without requesting manual reports from each property's facility manager.
needs.satisfactionRationale: No automated compliance status reporting exists in any incumbent system. All compliance communication requires manual report generation and delivery.

needs.statement: Minimize the likelihood of compliance documentation quality varying significantly between properties within a portfolio
needs.importanceRationale: Inter-property documentation variance creates audit risk and undermines portfolio-level compliance assurance for asset managers and insurers.
needs.satisfactionRationale: Manual documentation by different property teams produces inherently variable quality. No standardized template or automated generation exists across portfolios.

needs.statement: Minimize the likelihood of 868 MHz signal attenuation through reinforced concrete floor slabs creating per-floor monitoring blackouts in multi-story commercial office buildings
needs.importanceRationale: Multi-story commercial buildings use reinforced concrete floor slabs that attenuate 868 MHz signals, creating floor-level monitoring blackouts.
needs.satisfactionRationale: Per-floor repeater infrastructure mitigates but adds cost and complexity. No predictive RF modeling exists for commercial office floor slab configurations.

needs.statement: Minimize the likelihood of IoT door-event timestamp collection triggering a GDPR Article 35 Data Protection Impact Assessment in EU commercial office deployments with employee-occupied areas
needs.importanceRationale: GDPR Article 35 DPIAs add procurement delay and legal review cost; triggering a DPIA can delay deployment by months in EU commercial office buildings.
needs.satisfactionRationale: Most exit monitoring systems collect anonymous event data. However, timestamp correlation with access control records may trigger DPIA requirements in employee-occupied areas.

needs.statement: Minimize the likelihood of on-site dispatch responses being required for non-emergency door activations that could be resolved remotely
needs.importanceRationale: On-site dispatch for non-emergency events is a significant cost driver in portfolio operations; remote resolution capability reduces operational expenses.
needs.satisfactionRationale: A3 SafeRoute provides some remote status visibility. A1/A5 require physical visit for every event. Remote resolution capability is limited across the market.

needs.statement: Minimize the likelihood of emergency exit alarm devices triggering false positives from authorized key-override access by facility personnel
needs.importanceRationale: False positives from authorized key-override create alarm fatigue and waste security dispatch resources.
needs.satisfactionRationale: Key-override mechanisms in all alternatives adequately suppress false alarms during authorized access. Satisfaction is moderate at 5/10.

needs.statement: Increase the ability to retrofit emergency exit security devices to existing commercial doors without structural modification
needs.importanceRationale: Structural modification in occupied commercial buildings requires tenant coordination, building permits, and creates disruption to business operations.
needs.satisfactionRationale: A1 mechanical alarms (40%) are surface-mount retrofit-friendly. A3 SafeRoute requires wiring but not structural modification. Market-weighted satisfaction is moderate.

needs.statement: Minimize the likelihood of unheated commercial stairwell and service corridor temperature variations reducing battery service life below the 2-year maintenance target
needs.importanceRationale: HVAC-dark zones (stairwells, service corridors) in commercial buildings experience temperature extremes that can reduce battery electrochemical performance.
needs.satisfactionRationale: Standard 9V batteries meet the 2-year target in conditioned spaces. HVAC-dark zone impact is uncertain but manageable with proactive monitoring.

needs.statement: Minimize the likelihood of wireless emergency exit monitoring commissioning requiring specialist RF survey support
needs.importanceRationale: Specialist RF survey adds commissioning cost and scheduling dependency; standard FM technicians should be able to commission without external support.
needs.satisfactionRationale: Walk-test procedures are available for most systems. Specialist RF survey is rarely required for standard commercial buildings. Satisfaction is moderate.

needs.statement: Minimize the likelihood of incorrect wireless address assignment causing two door guardian units on adjacent doors to share a transmission channel
needs.importanceRationale: Address collision causes event misattribution between adjacent doors, undermining door-level event accuracy and compliance records.
needs.satisfactionRationale: Learn-mode pairing procedures prevent address collision in most systems. The risk is low and manageable with standard commissioning procedures.

needs.statement: Minimize the likelihood of battery replacement in emergency exit monitoring devices requiring specialist skills or triggering false alarm events
needs.importanceRationale: Battery replacement by standard building staff without false alarm activation reduces operational cost and response overhead.
needs.satisfactionRationale: Key-bypass during battery replacement is standard. The process is manageable for trained building staff. Satisfaction is adequate.

needs.statement: Minimize the likelihood of inconsistent alarm activation thresholds causing premature or late alarm triggers across door types with different handle configurations
needs.importanceRationale: Inconsistent thresholds across lever-handle and panic-bar variants create unpredictable alarm behavior within the same building.
needs.satisfactionRationale: Mechanical alarms have consistent trigger force by design. Surface-mount guards may vary slightly across door types. Overall consistency is acceptable.
